news_summary: Scientists have successfully completed Schrödinger’s century-old color theory, marking a significant breakthrough in understanding color science.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont parvenu à compléter la théorie des couleurs de Schrödinger, vieille d'un siècle, marquant ainsi une avancée majeure dans la compréhension de la science des couleurs.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado completar la teoría del color de Schrödinger, que data de hace un siglo, lo que supone un avance significativo en la comprensión de la ciencia del color.

articles.title: Scientists finally complete Schrödinger’s 100-year-old color theory
articles.summary: Researchers have finally resolved a key problem in a 100-year-old theory of color, showing that the qualities we perceive in colors are intrinsic to the mathematics of color space itself. The discovery sharpens our understanding of human vision and could lead to more precise color technologies and visualizations.
articles.content:
A century old idea from Erwin Schrödinger has taken a major step forward, thanks to new research into how humans perceive differences between colors.

A team led by Los Alamos scientist Roxana Bujack used geometry to build a mathematical definition of color perception based on hue, saturation, and lightness. Their results, presented at a visualization science conference, formalize Schrödinger's model of color and show that these familiar color qualities are built into the structure of color perception itself.

"What we conclude is that these color qualities don't emerge from additional external constructs such as cultural or learned experiences but reflect the intrinsic properties of the color metric itself," Bujack said. "This metric geometrically encodes the perceived color distance -- that is, how different two colors appear to an observer."

Completing Schrödinger's Color Puzzle

By defining these perceptual attributes more rigorously, the researchers have supplied a missing piece in Schrödinger's long standing vision for a closed mathematical model of color. The goal was to define hue, saturation, and lightness using only the geometric property of highest color similarity.

Human color vision is based on three types of cone cells, which are centered around red, blue, and green. That gives color spaces three dimensions, allowing scientists to organize and compare colors mathematically.

In the 19th century, mathematician Bernhard Riemann proposed that perceptual color spaces are not flat or straight, but curved. In the 1920s, Schrödinger built on that idea by defining hue, saturation, and lightness within a Riemannian model of color perception, using a metric that describes how people perceive color differences.

Fixing a Century Old Mathematical Gap

Schrödinger's definitions have shaped color science for roughly 100 years. But while the Los Alamos team was developing algorithms for scientific visualization, they found that the mathematics behind the model had important weaknesses.

The biggest problem involved the neutral axis, the line of grays that runs from black to white. Schrödinger's definitions of hue, saturation, and lightness depend on where a color sits in relation to that axis, yet he never formally defined the axis itself.

That omission created a serious gap. Without a precise definition of the neutral axis, the entire construction was formally incomplete. The team's most important advance was finding a way to define the neutral axis using only the geometry of the color metric.

To accomplish that, the researchers had to move beyond the traditional Riemannian model. That shift represents a major mathematical advance for visualization science.

A Better Model of How Colors Change

The team also corrected two other important issues in the older framework.

One involved the Bezold- Brücke effect, a phenomenon in which changing light intensity can make a color appear to shift in hue. The researchers addressed this by using the shortest path in their geometric model of color perception rather than relying on a simple straight line.

They also used the shortest path in a non-Riemannian space to account for diminishing returns in color perception, another effect that had not been fully captured by the older approach.

Why Color Perception Matters

The research was presented at the Eurographics Conference on Visualization and builds on a broader Los Alamos project on color perception. That project also produced a groundbreaking 2022 paper in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

A more precise model of color perception could have wide value in fields that depend on accurate color, including photography, video, visualization, and related technologies. It could also improve the way scientists create and interpret visual data.

Scientific visualization plays an important role in helping researchers understand complex information. Better color models can support more effective analysis across many areas, including national security sciences.

The team's work now provides a foundation for future color modeling in non-Riemannian space.

Funding: This work was supported by the Laboratory Directed Research and Development program at Los Alamos and by the National Nuclear Security Administration's Advanced Simulation and Computing program.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in completing a century-old mathematical model of color perception, which sharpens understanding of human vision and has broad applications in color technologies, scientific visualization, and data interpretation. The impact is meaningful for many fields relying on accurate color representation, and the research provides a solid foundation for future advances.
articles.personality_title: Scientists solve century-old puzzle about how we see color
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For about 100 years, scientists have worked to understand how we see colors. A famous idea by scientist Erwin Schrödinger tried to explain color perception using math, but it had a missing piece.

**What happened** – A team led by Roxana Bujack at Los Alamos National Laboratory found the missing piece in Schrödinger’s color theory. They used geometry to clearly define hue, saturation, and lightness—the qualities we notice in colors—by focusing on the neutral axis, the line of grays from black to white, which Schrödinger never fully defined.

**Impact** – This discovery completes a key part of a 100-year-old theory, showing that the way we see colors comes directly from math, not from culture or learning. It improves how scientists understand color differences and can make color-based technologies like photography and scientific visualizations more accurate.

**What's next step** – The team’s new math model moves beyond older methods and will help future research in color perception. It can lead to better tools for scientists who rely on precise color data, especially in complex fields like national security and data analysis.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have solved a long-standing math problem, improving how we understand and use color in science and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques résolvent un mystère centenaire sur la perception des couleurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis environ 100 ans, les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment nous percevons les couleurs. Une célèbre théorie d’Erwin Schrödinger tentait d’expliquer la perception des couleurs avec des mathématiques, mais une pièce manquait.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Roxana Bujack au laboratoire national de Los Alamos a trouvé cette pièce manquante dans la théorie des couleurs de Schrödinger. Ils ont utilisé la géométrie pour définir clairement la teinte, la saturation et la luminosité—les qualités que nous remarquons dans les couleurs—en se concentrant sur l’axe neutre, la ligne de gris allant du noir au blanc, que Schrödinger n’avait jamais vraiment défini.

**Impact** – Cette découverte complète une partie clé d’une théorie vieille de 100 ans, montrant que notre perception des couleurs vient directement des mathématiques, et non de la culture ou de l’apprentissage. Elle améliore la compréhension des différences de couleurs et peut rendre les technologies basées sur la couleur, comme la photographie et les visualisations scientifiques, plus précises.

**Prochaine étape** – Le nouveau modèle mathématique de l’équipe dépasse les méthodes anciennes et aidera les futures recherches sur la perception des couleurs. Il pourra conduire à de meilleurs outils pour les scientifiques qui dépendent de données précises sur les couleurs, notamment dans des domaines complexes comme la sécurité nationale et l’analyse de données.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont résolu un problème mathématique ancien, améliorant notre compréhension et notre utilisation des couleurs en science et technologie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos resuelven un enigma centenario sobre cómo vemos el color
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante aproximadamente 100 años, los científicos han tratado de entender cómo percibimos los colores. Una teoría famosa del científico Erwin Schrödinger intentó explicar la percepción del color con matemáticas, pero faltaba una parte importante.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Roxana Bujack en el Laboratorio Nacional de Los Álamos encontró esa parte faltante en la teoría del color de Schrödinger. Usaron geometría para definir claramente el tono, la saturación y la luminosidad—las cualidades que notamos en los colores—centrándose en el eje neutro, la línea de grises que va del negro al blanco, que Schrödinger nunca definió completamente.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento completa una parte clave de una teoría de 100 años, mostrando que la forma en que vemos los colores viene directamente de las matemáticas, no de la cultura o el aprendizaje. Mejora cómo los científicos entienden las diferencias de color y puede hacer que tecnologías basadas en color, como la fotografía y las visualizaciones científicas, sean más precisas.

**Próximo paso** – El nuevo modelo matemático del equipo va más allá de los métodos antiguos y ayudará a futuras investigaciones sobre la percepción del color. Puede conducir a mejores herramientas para científicos que dependen de datos precisos sobre colores, especialmente en campos complejos como la seguridad nacional y el análisis de datos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Investigadores resolvieron un problema matemático antiguo, mejorando cómo entendemos y usamos el color en la ciencia y la tecnología.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned geometric landscape of interconnected color spheres and ribbons representing hue, saturation, and lightness, arranged around a glowing central neutral axis line from deep black to bright white, with subtle curved paths illustrating color perception shifts, all rendered as a detailed, softly textured painting using natural, muted colors.
